story:
Cold. Hissing. Sirens. My ears ring until the cryo pod cracks open and drops me to the floor. A looped voice shrieks, “CRITICAL CRYOGENIC MALFUNCTION. PLEASE EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY.” For a cat, it’s basically torture. The lab is empty—no scientists, no guards, no other test subjects. Doors locked, windows unbreakable, vents too high.

Then I remember the command terminal. Even when main power’s down, they run on backup. I hop up, skim the options—View Cameras, Open Doors (greyed out), Print Keycard, Open Emergency Exit (locked to admin)—and hit Print Keycard. The printer coughs up my freedom. I grab my wrist rig—still clipped to the cryo rack—boot the offline app I coded myself (yes, I’m a genius), and sync with the terminal.

The date punches me in the gut: October 3, 2111. Thirty-six years gone. No family to miss me, no friends except a hand-stitched yellow bunny plush named Gerard. I swipe the new card, the door hisses, and I step into a facility that should be full of human noise. I need to get three floors up to reach the emergency elevator—the only shaft that still pulls power.

A shadow flickers. I slip into the vehicle workshop and arm up: a short pry bar, a shard of metal, paracord from my old backpack. I lash the shard to the bar into a cat-length spear. There’s a sealed bottle of stale-tasting water, bandages, spare parts—things I left behind like a time capsule.

Movement again. A scent. Cat.

She hits me fast. We clash—she’s quick, I’m desperate—and my blade scores her chest. She doesn’t fold. We grapple until other cats’ scents flood in. A voice cuts through: “That’s enough, Mint Nose!” My attacker freezes. She’s a dappled tortoiseshell with pale green eyes and a white nose that is annoyingly cute. Another she-cat, older and steadier, steps out.

“Greetings, Clover Leaf,” Mint Nose says.

Clover Leaf studies me. “A new cat. Not from Radiance or the Collective.”

Radiance? The Collective?

They question me. I admit I came from “a room, right over there,” but not by choice. Clover Leaf offers to show me out and warns me to avoid the waterworks and Route 66. At the surface, a tom and another cat wait for us. I wrap Mint Nose’s wound with my bandages. She winces, thanks me. Clover Leaf invites me to visit their territory—maybe even join them. They melt into the ruins.

Only then do I really look at the city.

The human bustle I remember is gone. Barricades, bases, vehicles—all scorched and broken. Whatever happened was bad. The other subjects are missing; maybe they slipped out when everything fell apart. Maybe those cats were their descendants. A shape shifts on a distant water tower. The warning about the waterworks and Route 66 echoes in my head.

“Hello?” An elder cat steps from the shadows, wearing an old dish towel like a shawl, scarred and calm. She calls the group I met the Ashclaws—rulers of the south with iron paws. Not enemies to everyone, but not welcoming either. They dislike weapons; I learned that fast.

She tells me fire rained from the sky. No one knows if it was a human accident or a war gone catastrophic. Humans returned eighteen years ago in armored columns and wiped out four of the seven cat factions, mistaking us for dangerous mutants, then pulled back. Now three major powers remain: Radiance, the Ashclaws, and the Rustpaw Collective. The Ashclaws’ victory gave them the stretch from the wall of cars on the eastbound highway all the way to Route 66. Cross their borders and you’re prey.

The sun bleeds vermilion. “Danger’s worse at night,” the elder says.

I ask why I should trust her.

“You don’t. End of the world means guessing who you trust.”

Fair. I follow her. A tank hatch creaks open, and we slip inside.
